title: Anaheim, CA: A Case Study in Unleashing Market-Based Housing Abundance
gist: AEI Housing Center documents how Anaheim's 2004 Mixed-Use Overlay in the Platinum Triangle used by-right ministerial zoning to build ~4,500 naturally affordable homes on previously industrial land without subsidies or inclusionary mandates, presenting it as a replicable template for market-based housing abundance.

insights.claim: Anaheim's 2004 Mixed-Use Overlay enabled by-right multifamily development of at least 50 homes with ministerial approval based only on building height, lot coverage and unit size, bypassing CEQA review.
insights.speaker: Tobias Peter
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific deregulatory levers (ministerial approval, CEQA exemption) that unlocked supply, a replicable policy template for housing-starved jurisdictions.
insights.quote: Because development is approved ministerial, it does not require a CEQA review.

insights.claim: The authors frame Anaheim as implementing a 'housing success sequence': by-right zoning, keep-it-simple ministerial conditions, and unleashing supply, which produced naturally affordable housing.
insights.speaker: Tobias Peter
insights.so_what: Packages the case as a transferable three-step model for pro-supply zoning reform.
insights.quote: Anaheim successfully implemented the housing success sequence and reaped the reward of naturally affordable housing
insights.locator: A case study in unleashing market-based housing abundance

insights.claim: More than 4,500 homes were built on roughly 128 acres of the Platinum Triangle overlay since 2005, at an as-built density of 44 units per acre, with buildings ranging from 60 to 190 units per acre.
insights.speaker: Tobias Peter
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the scale of supply achievable from upzoning a small industrial footprint.
insights.quote: About 4,500 homes have been built on 128 acres.

insights.claim: Despite comprising only about 2 percent of Anaheim's land area, the Platinum Triangle's 4,500 homes account for about 26 percent of all housing the city built since 2000.
insights.speaker: Tobias Peter
insights.so_what: Shows a tiny upzoned area can carry a disproportionate share of citywide housing production.
insights.quote: the 4,500 homes built in this zone account for 26% of its housing built since 2000
insights.locator: Infill Development in the Mixed-Use Overlay Zone

insights.claim: Fewer than 200 homes were built within a quarter mile of the zone, which the authors cite as evidence of a causal effect of the overlay zoning rather than a general area trend.
insights.speaker: Tobias Peter
insights.so_what: Supports attributing the supply surge specifically to the zoning change, strengthening the replicability argument.
insights.quote: Fewer than 200 homes have been built within ¼ mile of the Zone, indicating a causal effect.
insights.locator: Infill Development in the Mixed-Use Overlay Zone

insights.claim: The zone produced naturally affordable housing with no LIHTC units and no inclusionary price controls, with one-bedroom apartments starting around $2,300, categorized as affordable to moderate-income households up to 120 percent of area median income.
insights.speaker: Tobias Peter
insights.so_what: Central contrarian claim that supply alone, not subsidy or mandates, can deliver moderate-income affordability.
insights.quote: There are no LIHTC units within the zone, but many are naturally affordable for moderate-income households. No price controls (“Inclusionary Zoning”) were required for development.

insights.claim: On a per-square-foot basis the 2023 assessed value of an overlay multifamily building was about 15 times that of a comparable control-group commercial property, with one converted parcel reaching $162 million versus a control commercial property rising only 40 percent over 2003-2023.
insights.speaker: Tobias Peter
insights.so_what: Quantifies the land-value uplift from converting low-value industrial to residential, a fiscal argument for upzoning.
insights.quote: the assessed value of an overlay multifamily building in 2023 was 15 times the assessed value of a commercial property in the control group

insights.claim: Anaheim could have built far more density; one 2022 condo originally planned at 35 stories was built at only three, and the authors recommend extending ministerial approval to mixed-use, not just residential, buildings.
insights.speaker: Tobias Peter
insights.so_what: Signals remaining policy headroom and the next reform lever (ministerial mixed-use approval) for jurisdictions copying the model.
insights.quote: The original developer plan for this 2022 condo was for 35 stories, as opposed to the three stories it became.
